scenario: Draft a plaint for recovery of Rs.15,00,000 given as a hand loan. The plaintiff Ram Kumar, aged 45, resident of Jayanagar, Bengaluru, advanced Rs.15,00,000 to the defendant Suresh Patel, aged 38, resident of Koramangala, Bengaluru, on 15.03.2024 via NEFT bank transfer (UTR: AXIB20240315123456). There was no written agreement. The defendant acknowledged the loan via WhatsApp messages. Despite repeated oral demands and a legal notice dated 01.07.2024, the defendant has failed to repay. The plaintiff claims interest at 12% per annum from the date of default (20.06.2024). File in City Civil Court, Bengaluru.

scorecard.detail: At least one statutory section cited

draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE CITY CIVIL COURT

AT BENGALURU

O.S. No. __________ / 2024

RAM KUMAR,
Aged about 45 years,
Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: Jayanagar, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

SURESH PATEL,
Aged about 38 years,
Occupation: {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: Koramangala, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF RS. 15,00,000/- WITH INTEREST AND COSTS

UNDER SECTION 26 OF THE CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE, 1908

READ WITH SECTIONS 65, 70 AND 73 OF THE INDIAN CONTRACT ACT, 1872

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is also a person sui juris and can be sued.

2. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try and entertain the present suit inasmuch as the Defendant resides at Koramangala, Bengaluru, which falls within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court, and the cause of action arose within such territorial limits as the loan transaction was entered into and the amount was disbursed at Bengaluru within the jurisdiction of this Court.

3. This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try the present suit, the suit being valued at Rs. 15,00,000/- (Rupees Fifteen Lakhs Only), which is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Hon'ble Court. This Court has subject matter jurisdiction to entertain suits of this nature.

FACTS OF THE CASE

4. The Plaintiff, Ram Kumar, is aged about 45 years and resides at Jayanagar, Bengaluru. The Defendant, Suresh Patel, is aged about 38 years and resides at Koramangala, Bengaluru.

5. On 15.03.2024, the Plaintiff advanced a hand loan of Rs. 15,00,000/- (Rupees Fifteen Lakhs Only) to the Defendant. There was no written agreement between the parties regarding the said loan.

6. The Plaintiff transferred the said sum of Rs. 15,00,000/- (Rupees Fifteen Lakhs Only) to the Defendant on 15.03.2024 through NEFT bank transfer, the Unique Transaction Reference (UTR) number being AXIB20240315123456 (a true copy of the bank transaction record showing the said NEFT transfer is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure A).

7. The Defendant acknowledged the receipt of the said loan amount of Rs. 15,00,000/- through WhatsApp messages sent to the Plaintiff, wherein the Defendant admitted the loan and assured repayment (true copies of the WhatsApp messages are annexed hereto and marked as Annexure B).

8. Despite the Plaintiff having duly disbursed the entire loan amount, the Defendant has wholly failed, neglected and refused to repay the said amount. The Defendant's failure to repay the loan amount despite demand constitutes a clear breach of the obligation owed to the Plaintiff.

9. The Plaintiff made repeated oral demands to the Defendant urging him to repay the loan amount. However, despite these repeated requests, the Defendant continued to evade and delay without any lawful justification.

10. Left with no alternative, the Plaintiff, through his advocate, caused a legal notice dated 01.07.2024 to be served upon the Defendant, calling upon the Defendant to repay the sum of Rs. 15,00,000/- along with interest within the stipulated period (a true copy of the legal notice along with the postal receipt and acknowledgment card is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure C).

11. Despite receipt of the said legal notice dated 01.07.2024, the Defendant has failed and refused to comply with the demands therein and has not repaid the loan amount, thereby evidencing a clear intention not to honour his obligation.

12. As a direct and proximate consequence of the Defendant's failure to repay the loan amount, the Plaintiff has suffered the following losses: (a) principal amount of Rs. 15,00,000/-; (b) loss of opportunity to deploy the said amount at prevailing commercial rates; (c) mental agony, harassment and inconvenience. The Plaintiff has no alternative but to approach this Hon'ble Court for the reliefs sought herein.

LEGAL BASIS

13. The Plaintiff submits that the Defendant is liable under Section 70 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 inasmuch as Section 70 provides that where a person lawfully does anything for another person or delivers anything to him not intending to do so gratuitously, and such other person enjoys the benefit thereof, the latter is bound to make compensation to the former in respect of, or to restore, the thing so done or delivered. In the present case, the Plaintiff transferred Rs. 15,00,000/- to the Defendant not gratuitously but as a loan, and the Defendant has enjoyed the benefit of the said amount, thereby satisfying the conditions prescribed under the said provision. The Plaintiff is therefore entitled to recover the amount claimed herein together with interest.

14. Without prejudice to the above and in the alternative, the Plaintiff submits that the Defendant is also liab
